tools.description: Close a GitHub issue with a closing comment. Use this when work is complete, the issue is no longer relevant, or it's a duplicate. The closing comment should explain the resolution or reason for closing. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be closed. Target: *.

body.description: Closing comment explaining why the issue is being closed and summarizing any resolution, workaround, or conclusion.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to close. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 901 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/901). If omitted, closes the issue that triggered this workflow (requires an issue event trigger).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

state_reason.description: The reason for closing the issue. Use 'COMPLETED' for resolved issues, 'NOT_PLANNED' for issues that won't be addressed, or 'DUPLICATE' for duplicate issues. Defaults to 'COMPLETED'.

tools.name: close_issue

tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Target: *.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Add labels to an existing GitHub issue or pull request for categorization and filtering. Labels must already exist in the repository. For creating new issues with labels, use create_issue with the labels property instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be added. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to add labels to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, adds labels to the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to add (e.g., ['bug', 'priority-high']). Labels must exist in the repository. This field is required — omitting it will cause a validation error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_labels

tools.description: Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be removed. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to remove labels from. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: remove_labels

tools.description: Assign one or more GitHub users to an issue. Use this to delegate work to specific team members. Users must have access to the repository.

assignee.description: Single GitHub username to assign. Use 'assignees' array for multiple users.

assignees.description: GitHub usernames to assign to the issue (e.g., ['octocat', 'mona']). Users must have access to the repository.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to assign users to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 543 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/543). If omitted, assigns to the issue that triggered this workflow.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: assign_to_user

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Post a routing comment @mentioning team owners on the triggering issue; bypasses safe-outputs mention neutralization

message.description: The comment body text without any @mentions or @ symbols

owners.description: Comma-separated GitHub usernames to notify, without the @ prefix (e.g. 'user1, user2, Azure/team-name')

tools.name: mention_owners

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

content.text: {"number":57910,"title":"[Tools] Docs: Azure.GeneratorAgent CHANGELOG and README gaps after MCP Server refactor","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `Azure.GeneratorAgent` **Service directory:** `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` **Triggered by:** PR #57134 (commit `82d240a1b`) by `@radhgupta` ## What Changed PR #57134 refactored `Azure.GeneratorAgent` from a CLI tool with `generate`/`migrate` commands into an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server + tools + skill architecture. Key additions: - `--mcp-server` mode serving 19 auto-discovered tools over stdio - `DeterministicFixRegistry` with 27 error-pattern-to-tool rules - 19 individual MCP tools (regex fixes, nullable annotations, code gen, finalization, etc.) - Skill-driven workflow via `.github/skills/sdk-migration/SKILL.md` - 93 new NUnit tests (243 total) ## Gaps Found - **CHANGELOG.md describes the old CLI architecture**, not the new MCP server architecture. It says "Added `generate` command" and "Added `migrate` command" — neither of these commands exist in the refactored codebase; the entry point is now `McpServerHost.RunAsync` serving tools over stdio. - **README "Getting started" section has no MCP server invocation instructions** — it shows only `dotnet build` but not how to run the server (`dotnet run -- --mcp-server`), how to register it in VS Code / Claude Desktop `settings.json`, or how to configure it for Copilot. - **README "Key concepts" tool count is inconsistent**: says "18 individual tools" in prose but the table lists only 16; the `src/Mcp/Tools/` directory contains 19 tool files. Three tools are absent from the table: `DiscoverProjectTool`, `GeneratedSnapshotTool`, and `AddCodeGenSuppressTool`. - **README has no "Authenticate the client" section** (or explicit note that no auth is required), which is required by the standard README template. 📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files #### File 1: `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/CHANGELOG.md` — **Edit** Replace the entire body of the `1.0.0-beta.1 (Unreleased)` entry with accurate content reflecting the MCP refactor: ```markdown # Release History ## 1.0.0-beta.1 (Unreleased) ### Features Added - Initial release of `Azure.GeneratorAgent` as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server for automating Azure SDK code generation and migration workflows. - Added `--mcp-server` mode that exposes 19 auto-discovered deterministic fix tools over stdio transport. - Added `DeterministicFixRegistry` with 27 rules that map build error codes and message patterns to specific tool invocations, covering field renames, type-pattern replacements, missing/obsolete using directives, nullable annotation fixes, and method call replacements. - Added MCP tools: `regex_replacement`, `add_using_directive`, `remove_using_directive`, `nullable_annotation_fix`, `batch_fix`, `build_and_classify`, `classify_errors`, `run_code_generation`, `validate_tsp_config`, `commit_iteration`, `pregen_cleanup`, `migrate_test_samples`, `finalize_migration`, `run_tests`, `rename_codegen_type`, `fetch_to_fromlro`, `discover_project`, `generated_snapshot`, `add_code_gen_suppress`. - Each tool supports both MCP (JSON over stdio) and in-process invocation for testing. - Skill-driven workflow: the [`sdk-migration`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/skills/sdk-migration/SKILL.md) skill doc drives the full migration pipeline by calling MCP tools directly. ### Breaking Changes - Initial release, no breaking changes. ### Bugs Fixed - Initial release, no bugs fixed. ### Other Changes - Added 243 NUnit tests covering all MCP tools, the deterministic fix registry, build output parsing, and error classification. ``` #### File 2: `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/README.md` — **Edit** **A. Fix tool count and add missing tools to the MCP Tools table.** Replace: ``` Each tool supports both MCP (JSON) and in-process invocation. ``` with: ``` Each tool supports both MCP (JSON) and in-process invocation. ``` (no change to sentence, but fix number in prose: change "18 individual tools" → "19 individual tools") Add the three missing rows to the MCP Tools table: ```markdown | `discover_project` | Discover the `.csproj` and output path for a given service directory | | `generated_snapshot` | Capture a snapshot of generated files before migration for diff comparison | | `add_code_gen_suppress` | Add `[CodeGenSuppress]` attributes to suppress specific generated members | ``` **B. Add MCP server startup and registration instructions to the "Getting started" section.** After the existing `dotnet build` block, add a new subsection: ```markdown ### Run as MCP server Start the server over stdio (used by Copilot and other MCP clients): ``` dotnet run --project sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent -- --mcp-server ``` #### Register in VS Code (Copilot) Add to `.vscode/mcp.json` (or your user-level `settings.json` under `"mcp"` → `"servers"`): ``` { "servers": { "generator-agent": { "type": "stdio", "command": "dotnet", "args": [ "run", "--project", "\$\{workspaceFolder}/sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent", "--", "--mcp-server" ] } } } ``` #### Register in Claude Desktop Add to `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` (macOS) or `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json` (Windows): ``` { "mcpServers": { "generator-agent": { "command": "dotnet", "args": [ "run", "--project", "C:\\git\\azure-sdk-for-net\\sdk\\tools\\Azure.GeneratorAgent", "--", "--mcp-server" ] } } } ``` ### Authenticate the client No authentication is required. The MCP server operates entirely on the local file system and does not call any Azure service endpoints. ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No test-backed snippets are required for this change. The Examples section already contains illustrative shell/JSON blocks that do not need to be injected from test files. ### Step 3: Verify README structure After the edits, the README must contain these sections in order. Check each: 1. **Getting started** — ✅ present; add the new "Run as MCP server" and "Authenticate the client" subsections (see Step 1B above). 2. **Key concepts** — ✅ present; fix tool count from "18" → "19" and add three missing rows to the table. 3. **Examples** — ✅ present. 4. **Troubleshooting** — ✅ present. 5. **Next steps** — ✅ present. 6. **Contributing** — ✅ present. ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order: 1. `dotnet build sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `dotnet format sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` No snippet injection (`Update-Snippets.ps1`) or API export (`Export-API.ps1`) is needed since this is an internal tool, not a public NuGet client library. ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above. > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24113532834/agentic_workflow) · ● 494.3K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/57910","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-08T02:03:11Z","updated_at":"2026-04-08T02:03:11Z"}

{"total_count":29,"incomplete_results":false,"items":[{"id":3712790964,"number":54411,"state":"closed","state_reason":"completed","locked":true,"title":"[BUG] - MCPToolDefinition does not support the project_connection_id property like in Python MCPTool class","body":"### Library name and version

Azure.AI.Agents.Persistent 1.2.0-beta.8

### Describe the bug

The class MCPToolDefinition has no way to set the `project_connection_id` property like we can on the [MCPTool class](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/python/api/azure-ai-agents/azure.ai.agents.models.mcptool?view=azure-python-preview) in python.

The `project_connection_id` is a property that is necessary when creating an agent that uses a MCP tool already created in Foundry nextgen.

Here are some examples in python:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/main/sdk/ai/azure-ai-projects/samples/agents/tools/sample_agent_mcp_with_project_connection.py#L48

```python
    tool = MCPTool(
        server_label="api-specs",
        server_url="https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp",
        require_approval="always",
        project_connection_id=os.environ["MCP_PROJECT_CONNECTION_ID"],
    )
```

I believe the `project_connection_id` is metadata, in that it does not need to be in the MCPToolResource (i.e. per thread run) thus why it could be in the MCPToolDefinition.

### Expected behavior

Be able to have extra dynamic parameters that can be passed to the REST API of Foundry to align with the Foundry nextgen platform.

### Actual behavior

Impossible to set the the property in any shape or form.

### Reproduction Steps

```csharp
new MCPToolDefinition("MCP-Server-Label", "MCP-Remote-URL")
```

MCPToolDefinition does not expose the `project_connection_id` property

### Environment

OS: Windows 11 25H2 26200.6584

Azure.AI.Agents.Persistent 1.1.0-beta.1

### Describe the bug

Since the latest release of Azure.AI.Projects for Azure Foundry integration VisualStudioCredential within Visual Studio 2022 no longer works.

- VisualStudioCredential authentication failed: Process "C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community\Common7\IDE\CommonExtensions\Microsoft\Asal\TokenService\Microsoft.Asal.TokenService.exe" has failed with unexpected error: TS003: Error, TS004: Unable to get access token.  'Consent between first party application '04f0c124-f2bc-4f59-8241-bf6df9866bbd' and first party resource '18a66f5f-dbdf-4c17-9dd7-1634712a9cbe' must be configured via preauthorization - applications owned and operated by Microsoft must get approval from the API owner before requesting tokens for that API. For potential mitigation and additional information go to the following url: 'https://aka.ms/vspreautherror''.

This use to work prior to the new SDK as authentication used https://management.azure.com//.default for scopes. The new SDK uses https://ai.azure.com/.default and this fails with the above message.
I can use Azure CLI Credentials and the application will work as it uses a different ClientID but our development and test environments current rely on DefaultAzureCredentials and Visual Studio with Azure Service Authentication.
This all worked fine prior to the upgrade to the Azure.AI SDKs and the new structure. The root appears to be the VS App GUID not be registered with the Azure ML Services.

1.1.0-beta.1 (2025-05-21) - Azure.AI.Agents.Persistent
1.0.0-beta.9 (2025-05-16) - Azure.AI.Projects

### Expected behavior

As before - VisualStudioCredentials is able to authenticate against https://ai.azure.com/.default using VS App ID when using the latest version of Azure.AI.Projects and Azure.AI.Agents.Persistent.

### Actual behavior

Any use of VisualStudioCredentials with the latest Azure.AI.Projects or Azure.AI.Agents.Persistent fails which makes the use of DefaultAzureCredentials difficult in test and development.

### Reproduction Steps

Use Visual Studio (VisualStudioCredentials) and the latest SDKs to access Azure AI Foundry

### Environment

Visual Studio 2022 - Enterprise or Community

# [Bug] Trace Context Not Propagating from Azure Service Bus to Azure Function Trigger with Application Insights Enabled

## Summary

We are observing broken trace correlation between Azure Service Bus messages and the Azure Function App triggered by those messages, despite having Application Insights instrumentation enabled.

This issue affects observability and distributed tracing in event-driven systems, where consistent context propagation is critical for debugging and telemetry correlation.

## Setup

- Azure SDK: `Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus`
- Azure Function App (triggered via Service Bus Queue)
- .NET 6 / .NET 8 (we’ve seen this in both setups)
- Application Insights SDK enabled via `services.AddApplicationInsightsTelemetry()`
- No custom Activity or diagnostic injection code
- Using standard Azure-hosted Function App with queue trigger binding

## Behavior

- The sending application publishes a message to the Service Bus with diagnostics headers (as verified in message properties).
- The Function App is triggered successfully.
- However, in Application Insights:
  - The Function App invocation appears as a **separate trace**, not as a child span of the original sender.
  - No linkage is shown between the Function execution and the message origin.

## What We Expected

- The Azure SDK and Function App integration should preserve and propagate trace context end-to-end.
- The Function execution should appear as a child span in the same trace started by the sending application.

## Why It Matters

This kind of broken trace propagation leads to:
- Incomplete observability in distributed systems
- Higher effort for debugging and postmortem
- Inconsistent correlation across services and telemetry sources

We’ve implemented manual workarounds in other projects using custom `Activity` injection and context forwarding, but this adds unnecessary boilerplate and isn't scalable.

## Additional Notes

- We're using `ServiceBusClient` and `ServiceBusSender` to send messages.
- No custom headers are stripped in transport.
- Function App uses default bindings and configuration, and AI is wired through typical `ILogger` usage.

---

Please let us know if this is a known bug or a setup issue — happy to share code samples or minimal reproducer if needed.

Thanks again to @christothes  and @mikhailshilkov for the prior guidance!


### Expected behavior

When a message is sent to Azure Service Bus using the Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus SDK with distributed tracing enabled (via Application Insights or OpenTelemetry), and the message is processed by an Azure Function App (triggered via Service Bus), the trace context should be preserved end-to-end.

Specifically:
- The Azure Function App invocation should appear as a child span of the original sender's trace in Application Insights.
- The `trace-id`, `parent-id`, and other correlation metadata should be automatically extracted and passed into the Function App context.
- The full operation (Sender → Bus → Function) should be visible as a single distributed trace

### Actual behavior

Although the Service Bus message is sent with trace context (verified via diagnostics headers or message properties), the Azure Function App that processes the message creates a **new, unrelated trace** in Application Insights.

Observed symptoms:
- The Function App invocation appears as an independent trace, not connected to the sending operation.
- No parent-child relationship is visible in Application Insights.
- The trace-id and span-id from the original message are not linked to the Function’s telemetry.

## 🎯 Agentic Issue Triage

**Summary:** This issue documents gaps in the `Azure.GeneratorAgent` package documentation following a refactor from a CLI tool (generate/migrate commands) to an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server architecture. Specifically, the CHANGELOG still describes the old CLI commands, the README is missing MCP server startup instructions, has an inconsistent tool count (16 listed vs. 19 actual), and lacks an authentication section.

<details>
<summary>📋 Issue Details</summary>

**Package:** `Azure.GeneratorAgent` (sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/)
**Affected API:** Documentation — CHANGELOG.md and README.md
**Scenarios:**
- CHANGELOG.md entry for `1.0.0-beta.1` references old `generate` and `migrate` CLI commands that no longer exist
- README "Getting started" section is missing MCP server invocation instructions (`--mcp-server` flag, VS Code and Claude Desktop registration)
- README "Key concepts" tool count says "18 individual tools" in prose but table lists 16; actual code contains 19 tool files (`DiscoverProjectTool`, `GeneratedSnapshotTool`, and `AddCodeGenSuppressTool` are absent from the table)
- README is missing an "Authenticate the client" section (required by standard template; should note no auth is required)

**Root ask:** Update CHANGELOG.md and README.md to accurately reflect the MCP server architecture introduced in PR #57134.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🔎 Debugging / Reproduction Notes</summary>

This is a documentation-only gap, not a code bug. The discrepancies can be verified by:
1. Comparing `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/CHANGELOG.md` against the actual code entry point (`McpServerHost.RunAsync`)
2. Counting files in `sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/src/Mcp/Tools/` (should be 19) and comparing to the README table
3. Checking the README for presence of `--mcp-server` flag usage examples and `mcp.json` registration snippets

**Suggested investigation steps:**
1. Review PR #57134 to confirm the full scope of the MCP refactor
2. Update `CHANGELOG.md` to replace old CLI command references with accurate MCP server feature descriptions
3. Update `README.md` to fix the tool count (18 → 19), add the three missing tool rows, add MCP server startup/registration instructions, and add an authentication note
</details>

<details>
<summary>🏷️ Label Confidence</summary>

- **Category:** `Client` — `Azure.GeneratorAgent` is a non-management, non-provisioning SDK tool in the `sdk/tools/` directory
- **Service:** `CodeGen` — CODEOWNERS maps `/sdk/tools/Azure.GeneratorAgent/` to `%CodeGen` (line 1203–1204); the CodeGen service label (color #e99695) is confirmed by reference issue #23940
- **Confidence:** High — The package path and CODEOWNERS mapping are unambiguous; reference issue #23940 confirms the Client + CodeGen label pair for generator-related issues
</details>
